meta.title: 11.1 Batch Processing
meta.description: MapReduce, Spark, and the batch processing paradigm. When batch is the right tool.

blocks.prompt: You need to analyze 1 year of web server logs (50TB) to find the top 100 most visited pages. A single machine would take days. How do you parallelize this?

blocks.content:
## MapReduce

The foundational batch processing paradigm. Split work across many machines.

**Map phase:** Each machine processes a chunk of data independently, emitting key-value pairs.
**Shuffle phase:** Group all values by key across machines.
**Reduce phase:** Each machine aggregates values for its assigned keys.

```
Input: 50TB of log files across 1000 machines

Map: Each machine extracts (page_url, 1) from its logs
Shuffle: Group by page_url
Reduce: Sum counts per page_url → (page_url, total_visits)
```

## Apache Spark

Successor to MapReduce. Keeps data in memory between operations (10-100x faster).

**Key concepts:**
- **RDD (Resilient Distributed Dataset):** Immutable, partitioned collection
- **DataFrame:** Structured data with schema (like a distributed table)
- **Lazy evaluation:** Transformations are planned, not executed until an action is called

## When to use batch processing

- Data is bounded (finite dataset: logs, database dumps)
- Latency tolerance is hours (daily reports, monthly analytics)
- Complex transformations (ML training, graph processing)
- Reprocessing historical data

## When NOT to use batch

- Real-time requirements (use stream processing)
- Small data that fits on one machine (use pandas/SQL)
- Simple aggregations (use your database directly)

Batch processing (MapReduce, Spark) parallelizes computation across many machines for bounded datasets. Spark is 10-100x faster than MapReduce by keeping data in memory. Use batch for large historical analysis; use streaming for real-time.

blocks.prompt: Module 11.2 covers stream processing — handling unbounded, real-time data.
